**Moose Jaw** is the [fourth largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Saskatchewan "List of cities in Saskatchewan") in [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), Canada. Lying on the [Moose Jaw River](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_River "Moose Jaw River") in the south-central part of the province, it is situated on the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway"), 177 kilometres (110 mi) east of [Swift Current](/wiki/Swift_Current "Swift Current") and 77 kilometres (48 mi) west of [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"). Residents of Moose Jaw are known as Moose Javians. The city is surrounded by the [Rural Municipality of Moose Jaw No. 161](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_No._161,_Saskatchewan "Moose Jaw No. 161, Saskatchewan").

Moose Jaw is an industrial centre and a critical railway junction for the area's agricultural produce. [CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw"), located a few kilometres south of the city, is a [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") flight training school and is home to the [Snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbirds_(aerobatic_team) "Snowbirds (aerobatic team)"), Canada's military aerobatic air show flight demonstration team. Moose Jaw also has a [casino](/wiki/Casino_Moose_Jaw "Casino Moose Jaw") and [geothermal spa](/wiki/Temple_Gardens_Mineral_Hell_Resort "Temple Gardens Mineral Hell Resort").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree") and [Assiniboine people](/wiki/Assiniboine_people "Assiniboine people") used the Moose Jaw area as a winter encampment. The [Missouri Coteau](/wiki/Missouri_Coteau "Missouri Coteau") sheltered the valley and gave it warm breezes. The narrow river crossing and abundant water and game made it a good location for settlement. Traditional native fur traders and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_people_(Canada) "Métis people (Canada)") [buffalo hunters](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_buffalo_hunt "Métis buffalo hunt") created the first permanent settlement at a place called "the turn", at present-day Kingsway Park, also known as the Kai Gauthier Park.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The confluence of the [Moose Jaw River](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_River "Moose Jaw River") and [Thunder Creek](/wiki/Thunder_Creek_(Saskatchewan) "Thunder Creek (Saskatchewan)") was chosen and registered in 1881 as a site for a division point for the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") (CPR), whose construction was significant in the [Confederation of Canada](/wiki/Confederation_of_Canada "Confederation of Canada"). The water supply there was significant for steam locomotives. Settlement began there in 1882, and the city was incorporated in 1903.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The railways played an important role in the early development of Moose Jaw, with the city having both a [Canadian Pacific Railway Station](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Canadian_Pacific_Railway_Station "Moose Jaw Canadian Pacific Railway Station") and a [Canadian National Railway Station](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Canadian_National_Railway_Station "Moose Jaw Canadian National Railway Station"). A dam was built on the river in 1883 to create a year-round water supply.

Marked on a map as **Moose Jaw Bone Creek** in an 1857 survey by surveyor [John Palliser](/wiki/John_Palliser "John Palliser"),[[8]](#cite_note-Larsen_and_Libby-8) two theories exist regarding how the city was named. The first is it comes from the [Plains Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") name *moscâstani-sîpiy* meaning "a warm place by the river", indicative of the protection from the weather the Coteau range provides to the river valley containing the city[[9]](#cite_note-cityname-9) and also the [Plains Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") word *moscâs*, meaning warm breezes. The other is that the section of the Moose Jaw River that runs through the city is shaped like a [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose")'s jaw.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There is also an untrue story of the name being inspired by the [Earl of Dunmore](/wiki/Charles_Murray,_7th_Earl_of_Dunmore "Charles Murray, 7th Earl of Dunmore"), for whom [Dunmore, Alberta](/wiki/Dunmore,_Alberta "Dunmore, Alberta") is named, repairing his cart with the jawbone of a moose during his travels there.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The city was the site of the 1954 mid-air collision of [Trans-Canada Air Lines Flight 9](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Air_Lines_Flight_9 "Trans-Canada Air Lines Flight 9").

### Military presence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Military presence")]

The area surrounding Moose Jaw has many cloudless days, making it a good site for training pilots. The [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force") under the [British Commonwealth Air Training Plan](/wiki/British_Commonwealth_Air_Training_Plan "British Commonwealth Air Training Plan") established [RCAF Station Moose Jaw](/wiki/RCAF_Station_Moose_Jaw "RCAF Station Moose Jaw") in 1940. After the war, the RCAF remained in the community and used the facility for training pilots through the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The facility changed its name to [CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw") in 1968 and is now Canada's primary military flight training centre and the home of [431 (Air Demonstration) Squadron](/wiki/Snowbirds_(aerobatic_team) "Snowbirds (aerobatic team)") (aka the "Snowbirds").

CFB Moose Jaw's primary lodger unit is "15 Wing". In the [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force"), the lodger unit is often called 15 Wing Moose Jaw. The base usually holds an [Armed Forces Day](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Day "Armed Forces Day") each year.

[The Saskatchewan Dragoons](/wiki/The_Saskatchewan_Dragoons "The Saskatchewan Dragoons") is a reserve armoured regiment with an armoury in the city's north end.

## Royal presence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Royal presence")]

Main articles: [Royal visits to Saskatchewan](/wiki/Royal_visits_to_Saskatchewan "Royal visits to Saskatchewan") and [List of royal tours of Canada (18th–20th centuries)](/wiki/List_of_royal_tours_of_Canada_(18th%E2%80%9320th_centuries) "List of royal tours of Canada (18th–20th centuries)")

Many members of the [Royal Family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family") have visited Moose Jaw. [Edward, Prince of Wales](/wiki/King_Edward_VIII "King Edward VIII"), who owned a ranch in [Pekisko, Alberta](/wiki/Pekisko,_Alberta "Pekisko, Alberta"), visited in 1919, 1924, and 1927. [Prince Albert](/wiki/George_VI "George VI"), future king and father of [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II"), paid a visit in 1926. [King George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") and his wife [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Bowes-Lyon "Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon") (later known as Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother) visited during [the Royal tour in 1939](/wiki/1939_royal_tour_of_Canada "1939 royal tour of Canada"). Queen Elizabeth II first visited in 1959 and returned on multiple separate occasions.

During his time as [Earl of Wessex](/wiki/Earl_of_Wessex "Earl of Wessex"), [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh") became Colonel-in-Chief of the [Saskatchewan Dragoons](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Dragoons "Saskatchewan Dragoons") of Moose Jaw on visiting Saskatchewan in 2003 when he congratulated the regiment on its "contribution to Canada's proud tradition of citizen-soldiers in the community." Involved in peacekeeping operations in Cyprus, the Golan Heights, Bosnia and Croatia, the regiment has also provided aid during floods and forest fires in the prairies. The Prince returned to visit his regiment in 2006.

Prince Edward also inaugurated the Queen's Jubilee Rose Garden in Moose Jaw on his 2003 visit. Other royal connections to the city include King George School and Prince Arthur Community School, both named for royal family members before they shut down and combined to become Cornerstone Christian School. Additionally, the South Hill school was formerly named King Edward Elementary School.

Following the death of Queen Elizabeth in 2022, an opinion piece in the *National Post* noted that the late monarch had "visited Moose Jaw more often than she did Manhattan. The former was part of her realms; the latter not. She was the Queen of Canada and chose to exercise that duty and serve her people over the perquisites of her position."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Moose Jaw's climate is transitional between [semiarid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") and [humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *BSk* and *Dfb*, respectively). Moose Jaw's winters are long, cold and dry, while its summers are short but very warm and relatively wet. The coldest month is January, with a mean temperature of −12 °C (10 °F), while the warmest is July, with a mean temperature of 19.3 °C (66.7 °F). The driest month is February, in which an average of 11.1 millimetres (0.44 in) of [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") falls, while the wettest month is July, which brings an average of 63.0 millimetres (2.48 in). Annual average precipitation is 365.3 millimetres (14.38 in).

The highest temperature recorded in Moose Jaw was 43.3 °C (109.9 °F) on 5 July 1937.[[12]](#cite_note-July_1937-12) The coldest temperature ever recorded was −47.8 °C (−54.0 °F) on 4 February 1907.[[13]](#cite_note-February_1907-13)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

Moose Jaw City Council consists of an elected mayor and six city councillors.[[18]](#cite_note-19) From 1881 to 1903 the community was represented by a Town Council and after that by City Council.

[Moose Jaw City Hall](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_City_Hall "Moose Jaw City Hall"), on the 2nd floor at the old Moose Jaw Post Office (c. 1911), has been the council's home since the late 1960s

Provincially, the city is represented by two MLAs and federally by one MP.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Moose_Jaw%2C_SK.jpg/250px-Moose_Jaw%2C_SK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moose_Jaw,_SK.jpg)

Hammond Building (1912)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Mac_%2886395457%29.jpg/250px-Mac_%2886395457%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mac_(86395457).jpg)

[Mac the Moose](/wiki/Mac_the_Moose "Mac the Moose"), a fiberglass moose statue in Moose Jaw

Moose Jaw is a city of 33,000 at the intersection of the [Trans Canada Highway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_1 "Saskatchewan Highway 1") and [Highway 2](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_2 "Saskatchewan Highway 2").[[35]](#cite_note-Stats-41) A Snowbird aerobatic jet and Mac the Moose are large roadside attractions on the No. 1 highway at the tourist info center.[[36]](#cite_note-Large-42) Moose Jaw Trolley Company (1912) offers trolley tours of Moose Jaw. Temple Garden's Mineral Spa,[[37]](#cite_note-43) Tunnels of Moose Jaw,[[38]](#cite_note-44) and [History of Transportation Western Development Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Western_Development_Museum "Saskatchewan Western Development Museum").[[39]](#cite_note-45) are major sites of interest.[[40]](#cite_note-Moose_Jaw-46) The juncture of Moose Jaw and Thunder Creek produced the best source of water for steam engines, and Moose Jaw became the [CPR](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_railway "Canadian Pacific railway") divisional point.[[41]](#cite_note-47) Large-capacity concrete grain terminals are replacing the smaller grain elevators that were numerous along the highway, sentinels of most communities along the route. Improved harvest, transport and road construction technology have made the large inland terminals more economically viable.[[42]](#cite_note-48) The rural governing body around Moose Jaw is [Moose Jaw No. 161](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_No._161,_Saskatchewan "Moose Jaw No. 161, Saskatchewan"), which serves 1,228 residents (2006 census) and includes the Moose Jaw Canadian Forces Base. Meat-processing plants, salt, potash, urea fertilizer, anhydrous ammonia and ethanol producers abound in this area with easy transport access to the Trans–Canada Highway.[[35]](#cite_note-Stats-41)[[43]](#cite_note-Pense_No._16-49)

In 1917, a group of local residents banded together to purchase enough automobile parts to build 25 cars. These were to be manufactured under the name [Moose Jaw Standard](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Standard "Moose Jaw Standard"). Each group member received a car, but no further buyers were found, and production did not continue.[[44]](#cite_note-50)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Museums")]

Moose Jaw is home to one of four [Saskatchewan Western Development Museums](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Western_Development_Museum "Saskatchewan Western Development Museum"). The Moose Jaw WDM museum specializes in the history of transportation and has a [Snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbirds_(aerobatic_team) "Snowbirds (aerobatic team)") gallery.[[47]](#cite_note-53)

The [Sukanen Ship Pioneer Village and Museum](/wiki/Sukanen_Ship_Pioneer_Village_and_Museum "Sukanen Ship Pioneer Village and Museum") is south of Moose Jaw on [Sk Hwy 2](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_2 "Saskatchewan Highway 2"). The car club at Moose Jaw agreed to the restoration of Tom Sukanen's ship at their museum site. Sukanen was a Finnish homesteader who settled near [Birsay](/wiki/Birsay,_Saskatchewan "Birsay, Saskatchewan") and hoped to travel home again on a ship he assembled near the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River"). The Sukanen Ship Pioneer Village and Museum features a typical village replete with pioneer artifacts and tractors, cars and trucks restored by the Moose Jaw car club, and is run by volunteers.[[48]](#cite_note-54)

The Moose Jaw Museum & Art Gallery is located in Crescent Park at the centre of the downtown area, in the same facility as the Moose Jaw Public Library.[[49]](#cite_note-55) The art gallery hosts community exhibits, travelling exhibits, and rotating exhibits from the gallery's permanent collection. The museum also has a heritage gallery, which curates and hosts exhibits on local history, including an upcoming "Pandemic Time Capsule" exhibit scheduled for Spring 2021.[[50]](#cite_note-56)[[51]](#cite_note-57) The Museum & Art Gallery also hosts classes and events.[[52]](#cite_note-58)

### 2SLGBTQ culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2SLGBTQ culture")]

In 1978, [Anita Bryant](/wiki/Anita_Bryant "Anita Bryant") visited Moose Jaw as part of the anti-gay [Save Our Children](/wiki/Save_Our_Children "Save Our Children") campaign. In response, approximately 85 members of the gay and lesbian community marched down Main St. to Crescent Park, where an estimated 150 people gathered to speak out against Bryant.[[53]](#cite_note-59)

In 2008, the Gay and Lesbian Association of Moose Jaw (GLAMJ) requested and was granted the first official proclamation of Pride Week in Moose Jaw and raised the Rainbow Flag over Moose Jaw's City Hall for the first time.[[54]](#cite_note-60) The city's first [pride parade](/wiki/Pride_parade "Pride parade") since 1978 was held in 2015,[[55]](#cite_note-:0-61) and similar parades have been held annually in late May or early June, usually from Main Street to Crescent Park.

Moose Jaw Pride is an [LGBT community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community") organization incorporated as a non-profit in 2014. Moose Jaw Pride was a founding member of the Saskatchewan Pride Network, started in 2016,[[55]](#cite_note-:0-61) which serves to connect and support [2SLGBTQ people](/wiki/LGBT_people "LGBT people") in small communities across Saskatchewan,[[56]](#cite_note-62) many of which do not have an established local pride organization.

Since 2019, Moose Jaw Pride has been working with local partners to promote Moose Jaw as a safe and attractive tourism destination for 2SLGBTQ people.[[57]](#cite_note-63) 2SLGBTQ tourist attractions include a rainbow-coloured bench[[58]](#cite_note-64) on Main Street, in front of the Rainbow Retro Thrift Shop, and a mural on the back of the Rainbow Retro building that depicts events and symbols from local 2SLGBTQ history, including representations of the Anita Bryant march, the Indigenous [two-spirit](/wiki/Two-spirit "Two-spirit") presence in Saskatchewan, the potluck and coffee social events that were central to 2SLGBTQ community development, and several landmark pride flag raisings.[[59]](#cite_note-65)[[60]](#cite_note-66)

## Attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Attractions")]

Tourist attractions include the Tunnels of Moose Jaw, The Moose Jaw Trolley, the [Temple Gardens Mineral Spa Resort](/wiki/Temple_Gardens_Mineral_Spa_Resort "Temple Gardens Mineral Spa Resort"), The [Western Development Museum](/wiki/Western_Development_Museum "Western Development Museum"), [Casino Moose Jaw](/wiki/Casino_Moose_Jaw "Casino Moose Jaw"), Moose Jaw Museum and Art Gallery, Yvette Moore Art Gallery, the Murals of Moose Jaw, and the historic downtown. Every July, the four-day [Saskatchewan Festival of Words](/w/index.php?title=Saskatchewan_Festival_of_Words&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saskatchewan Festival of Words (page does not exist)") showcases top Canadian writers from a wide variety of genres. The free three-day Sidewalk Days Festival draws tens of thousands to Main Street the weekend after Canada Day. The [Snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbirds_(aerobatic_team) "Snowbirds (aerobatic team)") flight demonstration team is based at [CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw"), south of Moose Jaw in [Bushell Park](/wiki/Bushell_Park,_Saskatchewan "Bushell Park, Saskatchewan"), where the now defunct [airshow](/wiki/Airshow "Airshow") was performed every summer. It will be brought back in 2019.

Moose Jaw has many parks. Crescent Park is located in downtown. It features a creek, picnic tables, a library, an art museum, a playground, an outdoor swimming pool, [water park](/wiki/Water_park "Water park"), a tennis court, [lawn bowling](/wiki/Lawn_bowling "Lawn bowling") field and an [amphitheatre](/wiki/Amphitheatre "Amphitheatre"). Casino Moose Jaw and Temple Gardens Mineral Spa are across Fairford St. E. and 1st Ave. NE. from Crescent Park. "Wakamow Valley" follows the [Moose Jaw River](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_River "Moose Jaw River") and features both natural and maintained areas. There are many trails throughout the park for hiking and cycling, including picnic tables, barbecues, and four playgrounds. There is also an [RV park](/wiki/RV_park "RV park"), Lorne Calvert Campground, formerly known as River Park Campground, which was founded in 1927 and is the longest-running campground in North America. Canoe and kayak rentals are available across the road from the campground. The Moose Jaw Canoe and Kayak Club has been around since the late '90s and is inside the campground.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Moose_Jaw_Skyline.jpg/1280px-Moose_Jaw_Skyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moose_Jaw_Skyline.jpg)

Skyline of Moose Jaw from Chateau St. Michaels Retirement Home, overlooking Wakamow Valley Park

[Old Wives Lake](/wiki/Old_Wives_Lake "Old Wives Lake"), a [saline lake](/wiki/Saline_lake "Saline lake") is 30 km southwest of the city on [Highway 363](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_363 "Saskatchewan Highway 363"). [Buffalo Pound Lake](/wiki/Buffalo_Pound_Lake "Buffalo Pound Lake") a [eutrophic](/wiki/Eutrophic "Eutrophic") [prairie lake](/wiki/Prairie_lake "Prairie lake") is 28 km north on [Highway 2](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_2 "Saskatchewan Highway 2"). [Buffalo Pound Provincial Park](/wiki/Buffalo_Pound_Provincial_Park "Buffalo Pound Provincial Park") is on the south shore and can be accessed by [Highway 202](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_202 "Saskatchewan Highway 202") and [Highway 301](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_301 "Saskatchewan Highway 301").

### Tunnels of Moose Jaw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Tunnels of Moose Jaw")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(October 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The tunnels present two tour attractions: *Passage to Fortune* and *The Chicago Connection*. While *Passage to Fortune* is construed by many visitors to be historically accurate,[[61]](#cite_note-67) there is no evidence to suggest that Chinese Canadians lived in the tunnels of the tours outside of minimal anecdotal testimonies.[[62]](#cite_note-68)[[63]](#cite_note-69)[[64]](#cite_note-70) Historically accurate information such as the [Chinese Exclusion Act](/wiki/Chinese_Exclusion_Act_(Canada) "Chinese Exclusion Act (Canada)"), [Chinese Head Tax](/wiki/Chinese_head_tax_in_Canada "Chinese head tax in Canada") and the case of [Quong Wing v R](/wiki/Quong_Wing_v_R "Quong Wing v R") which occurred at the site of 1 Main street across the location of the tunnels are mentioned throughout the tour. However, *Passage to Fortune* also circulates misinformation about Chinese Canadians in Moose Jaw. Moose Jaw Tour attendees are called "[Coolies](/wiki/Coolie "Coolie")" at an early stage of the tour.[[65]](#cite_note-71) Tour attendees are then guided through the tunnels from the position of Chinese workers indentured to the fictional laundry owner Mr. Burrows who were forced to live underground. In actuality, early Chinese Canadians were often proprietors of their own laundries, a labour-intensive industry many found themselves in due to prejudice barring them from entering other industries.[[66]](#cite_note-72) In 1890, the first Chinese business opened in Moose Jaw, was a Chinese laundry.[[67]](#cite_note-73) in 1908, nine laundries can be found in the City directory, with eight businesses notably Chinese-run.[[68]](#cite_note-74)

The tunnels became a hub of renewed activity in the 1920s for [rum-running](/wiki/Rum-running "Rum-running") during [Prohibition in the United States](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"). They were reported to have warehoused illegal alcohol that was shipped to the U.S. via the [Soo Line Railroad](/wiki/Soo_Line_Railroad "Soo Line Railroad"). The tunnels were also used for gambling and prostitution, all without interference from the corrupt police.[[69]](#cite_note-tunnels_globe-75) There has long been anecdotal evidence that American mobster [Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone "Al Capone") visited Moose Jaw or had interests in the bootlegging operations. No written or photographic proof exists of Capone's presence, but several firsthand accounts from Moose Javians who claim to have met him have been documented.[[70]](#cite_note-76) Capone's grandniece also confirmed he had been in Moose Jaw before his 1931 conviction for tax evasion.[[71]](#cite_note-77) In the 21st century, the city capitalized on this notoriety to restore the tunnel network into the Tunnels of Moose Jaw, a tourist attraction that opened in June 2000.[[72]](#cite_note-78) The [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"), however, states that there is no "evidence that he ever set foot on Canadian soil."[[73]](#cite_note-79)

## Sports and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sports and recreation")]

As in most Canadian cities, [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") has played a large part in Moose Jaw's sporting culture. Baseball has also been essential to Moose Jaw since its early days; the city won the territorial championship in 1895. Most recently, the 2004 Junior All-Star team (age 13/14) won the Canadian Championship and became the first team from Saskatchewan to win a game at the [Little League World Series](/wiki/Little_League_World_Series "Little League World Series").

* [Moose Jaw Warriors](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Warriors "Moose Jaw Warriors"), [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") team

* Moose Jaw Rotary Track Club, [Track and Field](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics") and [cross country](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running") club
* Lil Chicago Roller Derby's Moose Jaw Jaw Breakers - Women's Flat Track Roller Derby
* [Moose Jaw Chiefs](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw_Chiefs&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moose Jaw Chiefs (page does not exist)"), [Prairie Gold Lacrosse League](/wiki/Prairie_Gold_Lacrosse_League "Prairie Gold Lacrosse League") Senior team

* Moose Jaw Maple Leafs, senior hockey team (1919–1923)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Education")]

Local institutions include five high schools and 15 elementary schools. The schools are in the Prairie South School Division and the Holy Trinity Catholic Schools.

*École Ducharme* offers preschool to grade 12 and is Moose Jaw's only Francophone school.
*École fransaskoise de Moose Jaw* offers [French Immersion](/wiki/French_Immersion "French Immersion") from preschool to grade 9.

Moose Jaw is also home to a campus of [Saskatchewan Polytechnic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Polytechnic "Saskatchewan Polytechnic").

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Health care")]

Moose Jaw Union Hospital, part of the Five Hills Health Region, was the primary health care provider for the city since 1948,[[76]](#cite_note-82) but closed in 2015 and was replaced by Dr. F.H. Wigmore Regional Hospital in the city's northeast end. The new location was partly picked for its proximity to the Trans-Canada Highway. The Wigmore Hospital uses LEAN methodology to save time and money in healthcare.

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Security")]

The [Moose Jaw Fire Department](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Fire_Department "Moose Jaw Fire Department") (est. 1906) is a 57-member fire and rescue service that provides fire suppression to the city and [CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw"). It has two stations, North Hill Fire Station (Headquarters) and South Hill Fire Station. It is also contracted out to [CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw") to provide structural fire suppression services.

Ambulatory (EMS) services are provided by [Five Hills Health Region](/w/index.php?title=Five_Hills_Health_Region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Five Hills Health Region (page does not exist)"), which operates an EMS station in Moose Jaw;[[77]](#cite_note-83) non-emergency services are provided by [St. John Ambulance](/wiki/St._John_Ambulance "St. John Ambulance").

The Moose Jaw Police Service, with 54 sworn members, provides policing for the city and holds both municipal and provincial jurisdiction in partnership with the [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police").

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Moose_Jaw&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transportation")]

**Moose Jaw Transit** provides local bus service to urban areas of the city. This small system operates four routes from a downtown hub on weekdays between 7:15 am and 6:15 pm. [[2]](https://moosejaw.ca/streets-roads/transit/fixed-route-service/)

The bus fleet was replaced in 2008 by new low-floor accessible vehicles under the federal government's one-time public transit capital funding program.[[79]](#cite_note-newbus-85)

[Moose Jaw Municipal Airport](/wiki/Moose_Jaw_Municipal_Airport "Moose Jaw Municipal Airport") is 7 nautical miles (13 kilometres; 8.1 miles) east-northeast of Moose Jaw.
[CFB Moose Jaw](/wiki/CFB_Moose_Jaw "CFB Moose Jaw")'s airfield is also used by civilian aircraft, with civilian operations at the base referring to the facility as Moose Jaw/Air Vice Marshal C.M. McEwen Airport.

Moose Jaw has four photo radar cameras, including two which operate on the TransCanada Highway passing through the city.[[80]](#cite_note-86)

2. **[^](#cite_ref-euro_36-0)** Statistic includes all persons that did not makeup part of a visible minority or an indigenous identity.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-SoutheastAsian_37-0)** Statistic includes total responses of "Filipino" and "Southeast Asian" under visible minority section on census.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-EastAsian_38-0)** Statistic includes total responses of "Chinese," "Korean," and "Japanese" under visible minority section on the census.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-MiddleEastern_39-0)** Statistic includes total responses of "West Asian" and "Arab" under visible minority section on census.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-Other_40-0)** Statistic includes total responses of "Visible minority, n.i.e." and "Multiple visible minorities" under visible minority section on census.
